Now this works really well for three distinct types of businesses and entrepreneurs. We're going to show you exactly how to do that. But first, let me show you some of the results that we've been able to get some of these clients in these different areas. This is actually selling t-shirts and Amazon products and membership subscriptions. These are all smaller ticket items, but when you add them up all together, it still makes 100 grants. He's absolutely crushing it, one of our top clients right now. Another client here spent $1,400 in ads to generate $125,000 in sales in one week. That was out of 242 leads and 13 applications and appointments. If those kind of numbers excite you, I'm glad because you're in the right place. We're going to show you exactly how we do this. We create a Facebook marketing machine that can target customers who are ready to buy. It can work with any budget. It gets Facebook to love you, and it's based on proven results. As you guys know, being here, you guys are sharp, savvy marketers. If you can't pay money to acquire a new customer, then you really don't have the ... That's what one of my mentors told me a few years ago, which really led me into a journey of finding out the most profitable paid traffic sources. The name of the game right here for me and with Facebook is to write small checks and then cash much bigger checks on the back end from selling your own product. Why Facebook? Well, Facebook is easy to set up. It's so easy. I'm going to show you here in just a few minutes how you can set it up with low budget and ad cost. You can start out with as little as $15 a day to test out. It is very easy and can scale very quickly. Now, Facebook is one of the largest social media, social network sites on the planet right now and it's had phenomenal growth. I mean, virtually everyone on the call, I'm guessing if you're not on Facebook, I would imagine that everybody here is. Facebook, there's over a billion users right now on Facebook. People spend, the average that they're spending is over six hours per month on Facebook and Facebook is continuing to grow more and more and more. Your customers are spending a lot of time on Facebook. If they're spending a lot of time on Facebook, it means that their eyeball is out there and you can be able to reach them very quickly and very easily. What does Facebook want? Well, number one thing that Facebook wants is engagement. If you look at how Facebook works and if we can reverse engineer and give what Facebook what it wants, then Facebook can give us as a business owner, as a marketer, Facebook can give us what we want. Facebook wants likes. It wants comments and it wants shares. It wants its users to be very active and it wants people to stay on Facebook as long as possible. The longer that they spend on Facebook, the more time that they spend on Facebook, then the more advertising they can do and they just build a stronger and stronger community. Now, Facebook also wants more advertisers, especially since they lend public care. Facebook has been wrapping up tremendously its ad platform over the past two years. Facebook really wants to take your money. They don't care if you make money back or not, if you're able to ROI your campaigns or not. Facebook doesn't care. Take your money just the same as anybody else's money. You do need to be careful, but by doing the things that we're talking about here today, you guys can set yourself up for success so that you can have the best chance to make not only your money back, but make a tremendous return on that money as well. Now, as a business owner, as a marketer, what do you want? Well, you want... Whoops, I'm getting a little bit of feedback here. Let's see, you want more customers. As a marketer, as a business owner, you want as many as possible. But one thing to be careful of when you're looking at Facebook advertising and Facebook marketing is that bigger is not always better. You don't want to go after 100 million people out there on Facebook. You want very targeted people that are going to respond to your offers, similar to some of the other lead generation that you've been learning here with Dave in this program, is you want to go after very targeted lists and very targeted demographics and coming down to, I know you guys have gone over your target customer and really getting clear about who is that target customer and building an avatar for that target customer and who they are. The cool thing with Facebook is that Facebook knows so much about us. We give them all of our likes and interests, and Facebook is taking all the information from the posts that people are making right now. We can take that from a marketer and from a business owner. We can be able to use that to target. It is like list building on steroids. It really is super targeted list creation that you don't have to pay a data broker or anything like that, or you just pay Facebook for it, and you can get some of the most targeted lists ever. When we talk about cracking the Facebook code, I use the analogy of a four digit lock here. You have to have all four things in place to be able to open up the keys to this treasure chest of wealth. The four things that we're going to be talking about here is number one, your audience, getting real there about who your audience is. Number two is ads and creating the ads and how to create them and what are the best types of ads to create. Number three is your landing page. The landing page that you're going to be setting up that I know you guys have already talked about in this program here with Dave, is setting up a simple landing page. Then number four is your conversion mechanism. Whatever conversion mechanism that is, if it is sales page, if it's a webinar, if it's a phone conversation, or if it's something as simple as having them come into your physical location and store. The first thing is targeting and figuring out the targeting for your ads. Facebook, there's over 180,000 people here in the US that you can target on Facebook. We can target by country. We can target by state, by city, even by zip code. For a lot of our national brands and international brands, we'll target the big five. We'll target US, Canada, UK, Australia, and New Zealand. For some of our local or regional clients, we'll target just in California. Or we'll target within a 25-mile radius of our physical therapist that's the Sound Street or Chiropractor. We can target people. We can go in and we can target by age and by gender. Then there are all sorts of different precise interests that we can use within Facebook based on the things that we like. This 180 million people then starts narrowing down to be a smaller and smaller and tighter demographic list. When it comes to the targeting, we want to be laser targeting. We don't want shotgun targeting. We want to be laser targeted because if we're shotgun targeting and throw it pasting a wide net, that means there's going to be a lot of waste. We want to go laser targeting into a very targeted group. Usually we want to have about 100,000 to 200,000 people to refine that list down to our top hyperactive customers that are going to respond the best to our messages. We can refine this list further down by demographics. We can refine it down by financials. If people own a home or their home value or even their net worth or their annual income, we can go down as far as that. We can go by job roles. We can target attorneys or we can target dentists or we can target financial professionals or whatever job role that you want to go after. We can target based on purchase history by auto purchase history of different types or makes of cars. We can go out and we can target BMW owners or Mercedes owners or whatever type of auto. If you're selling cars, there's some fantastic stuff in here that Facebook gives you to really be able to target people. We can target by retail purchase history, by types of business, by occupation, by financials, by lifestyle and interest. There's so many things that we can go in through because Facebook has partnered with three of the largest data providing companies in the world, Axiom, Datalogix and Epsilon. They have taken this data and layered it on top of all the data that Facebook already has so that we can really go deep in terms of who we are looking for. When you're thinking about your target customer avatar, you want to start thinking about what are the types of Facebook fan pages that my target customer would be liking? What are the types of Facebook groups that my target customer would be liking? What are the types of associations that they would belong to? What are the types of magazines that they subscribe to? What are the types of books that they read? What are the types of authors that they follow? Events that they go to? Are there any shows or programs or channels that they watch? What countries or states or cities or zip codes? Who are your top competitors that are in the space? Maybe they have some fan pages that you could then target. I have a lot of people that come up to me and they say, hey, I want to target so and so's fan page. They have 100,000 fans and I say, great, that's easy. Let's go on Facebook and let's create an ad that targets that fan page and you just type that in. Then we can target all those fans that they have built up. We can also go after similar products or services. I'll give you an example. One of our clients right now is a country music fan. They're about to launch a new music video and they're selling albums and they're selling t-shirts and merchandise and tickets. Then we're going to go out and we're targeting things like Duck Dynasty and other country music singers that are very similar to them and people that have similar interests. You can do the same thing if you are in the service business, let's say you're a chiropractor, you're going after, you could type in people who have back pain or neck pain within a 25 mile radius. If you really want to just be thinking about where do they hang out and what's the conversation going on right now on their head? What's the pain point? That's exactly where we can go on Facebook to be able to meet them and to be able to target our ads right to them. The second thing after you have your audience is now we're going to add creation. Add creation in Facebook is really easy. Facebook has an ads manager that you log into and they walk you through the whole concept here where you can add up to six different images. What it is is there's an image. Facebook is so image driven. Image is just pop on Facebook. You have an image that will stand out and then you have a headline and then you have a call to action and then a tracking link. These are the four things that you need in order to create your ad. The biggest thing that makes the most difference is your image. Having a good image that can pop out because that's what's going to grab people's attention. It's kind of like a billboard or placing a billboard right in the news feed on Facebook and as they're scrolling through their friends and family and picture the barbecue and things like that, they see an image that grabs them. Then that's going to make them want to click on that link and then go over to your landing page with exactly your message and where you're at. The best ads that we're creating are what are called promoted page post ads. If you look at this, this is an ad from the Trunk Club that I snapped the other day here. What it has is it has their fan page. You do need to have a Facebook fan page for your business. In this case, the name of their fan page is Trunk Club. They've got a little picture of a trunk in their profile picture. These types of ads are much cheaper than normal right hand side bar ads. They also get higher click through rates. They get more people that are engaging and actually clicking on the links over to your website. If you look at this ad here, they have a link that says become the best risk guy in the room. If you haven't already signed up for the Trunk Club, get started here. Then it has a tracking link. They're tracking and they know exactly how many clicks are coming over and they can track the source of where those conversions are coming from. That's really important so we can know what's working and what it's working. There's also, Facebook also puts a little like page up on the top right of your ad so that you can start getting more likes to your page while you're advertising. The real goal here in my opinion is to be generating leads and generating sales. Likes on a fan page is a really nice side benefit but it's not the primary objective when we're advertising in Facebook. The primary objective when we're advertising is to get leads and to make actual sales. That like is really nice bonus but we're really going for, we want people to go to the landing page that you've already set up. The cool thing about these promoted page posts, if you look down at the bottom of this line, is it has 4,891 likes, 369 comments and 543 shares. This is massive social proof where people are liking this. They're liking this advertising. They're commenting about it. They're commenting about the Trunk Club and then they're sharing it with your friends. We have seen that when we create these messages, we see the same results with our ads and with our clients ad where they're not only responding to it and actually filling out a lead application or buying something but they're also liking it and helping us share this message with their friends and commenting. Every time that they comment, like or share, Facebook accelerates those ad impressions. Now it goes out in front of more people than it did before. Facebook also sees that, hey, if this is getting a really high engagement on this ad, there's lots of people who are liking, commenting and sharing, that Facebook has been a lower your advertising costs so that it's going to be cheaper and cheaper and cheaper for you to run these ads. It's actually in your best interest to get as much interaction and engagement as you can on these ads and for you to actually be going in there or having someone on your team that's replying to these comments and replying to the engagement, that just adds more and more social proof. Now one thing that you want to look at when you're placing your ads is that Facebook allows you to target where you want your ads shown. You can show them on desktop computers. You can show them on mobile devices. You can show them on iPads or iPhones or Android devices. You can go down specifically by device and target by device. Now it depends on what kind of offer that you're running, but we have seen most of our success and our clients' success come through from desktop advertising. We also see good success with mobile advertising, but we tell our clients to start with only targeting desktop first. Then once you get desktop conversions working, then you can add in mobile and get mobile to work first, but you've got to make sure that you have mobile responsive websites, et cetera. I mean, sometimes it's going to be hard for people to fill out all their credit card information and billing information if they're just on their phone. So that's why we target desktop first. And iPads actually, we've seen the iPad devices work really well similar to desktops as being some of the highest profitable. So that's a big nugget for you guys. If you can just focus on that, focus on where your ads are being shown, because if you don't target specifically, then Facebook's just going to show it everywhere. Again, so the more targeted and specific that we can get with Facebook, the better results that we're going to be able to get. Now Facebook also has what's called the Power Editor, which is where we create most of our ads. And don't worry, it's not really too complicated or anything scary. It just allows you to make some of these refinements on your ads that I'm talking about here in terms of where you want your ads to show. If you want it to show, you can show it on the news feed on desktop or mobile. You can show it on desktop only, mobile only, the right hand column, and the news feed on desktop only or the right hand column only. And then you can also create custom audiences in the Facebook Power Editor where you can upload to if you already have a list of customers or an email list or a database, you can upload that list into Facebook of emails or phone numbers. And Facebook will match it with its database and give you typically, we get about a 50% match rate on as we upload these custom audiences. But then now not only are you emailing or mailing your customers, but you can also be targeting them on Facebook. So you're hitting them from multiple channels and multiple angles. The other thing that's really important on Facebook is to make sure that you have your converse tracking set up and in place. And there's a little bit of code that you can create for each goal that you have on your website. So if you have a lead or a buyer or an application, then you can track back to which ad that that lead or sale or application actually came from. So you can know exactly which ads are converting, what headlines are converting, what images are converting, and Facebook will track all of that for you right inside your ads. And then you can run reports and really start drilling down and seeing which ones are working and which ones aren't. And then you pause the losers and scale winners. The third thing that you can do on Facebook is what's called retargeting. And retargeting is like boomerang advertising. And you can do this using, there's two companies, there's Perfect Audience or Ad Roll, are the two companies. Or you can also do it right within Facebook right now too and create what's called a custom website audience right within Facebook. Now what you do is you place a little bit of code on all of the pages on your website and then anyone that comes to your website, you can run ads specifically to them through retargeting on Facebook and off Facebook as well if you use Perfect Audience or Ad Roll. We did that recently with one of our clients. They spent $206 in retargeting ads and that generated over $41,200 for them in sales. So retargeting is some of the easiest money, the lowest-paying fruit that you can get with Facebook advertising right now. So if you don't do anything else but just put a retargeting code on your website and start running some retargeting ads, you can get an immediate bump and see immediate results just from doing that with retargeting. So after you have your audience and then your ad, the third thing that we need is a landing page. And I know that Dave's went over this with you guys here in this program and setting up your landing page and I believe that you guys already have those all set up. So I'm not going to spend a whole lot of time on landing pages. One of the software that we use is called Lead Pages to create a lot of our landing pages. It's a really easy software where you can just set it up all online and you just type in the words that you want me connected to your autoresponder. So it's a great software that we use for landing pages. But you guys can use whatever landing pages that you've already set up will work just fine. By looking at your landing pages, your landing page is one of the most important pages on your website and it's targeted for each visitor and each audience that you want to go after. So for example, if we're going after dentists and we're going after chiropractors and we're going after real estate agents, we will probably want to have three different landing pages that one that speaks to dentists, one that speaks to chiropractors, and then one that speaks to real estate agents so that they don't all go to the same place but that we're specifically talking to those individual groups that we're coming from. But landing page optimization, you know, by testing and seeing what's working, whether it's switching out the headline or split testing the different design elements, we were working on that. So we were able to take one of our clients lead costs recently. We started out at $13.54 cost per lead and we're able to get it down to $1.36 per lead just by split testing different headlines and layouts on the landing page and refining that more. And that is a typical range that we're seeing as far as cost per lead from Facebook lead generation. We're seeing anywhere for US traffic, we're seeing anywhere from $3 to $5 is the cost per lead on average. You know, sometimes we'll get lower like here and you know, in the dollar range or $2 range, sometimes it'll be a little bit higher just depending on what audience that we're going after. But that's typically on average consistently generating leads for anywhere from $3 to $5. So if you can generate an unlimited amount of leads using Facebook and it costs you $3 to $5 per lead, then the question is how much does that lead work to you in terms of what your conversion mechanism is and what you're selling? And then how many do you want? You know, how many do you want to start getting? But there's a lot that you can get. Again I mentioned that the conversion tracking, this is the pixel that you place on the thank you page of you after they opt in or after they fill out a form on that landing page. You want to set up that conversion tracking so then you can know which ad is in place. You do that simply by going into your Facebook ads manager and on the left hand side there's a button that says conversion tracking and you create a new pixel and you can create a tracking pixel for checkout so people that are buying, you can create a pixel for a registry or ad to card or key page views or other website conversions. We typically do like leads or registrations or checkouts. So leads that they're just opting in, registrations if we're doing like a webinar and then checkouts if they're actually buying something on our funnel. So you can set up that conversion tracking if you don't know, if you're not techie or anything you can send this conversion pixel over to whoever is managing your website and they can do it for you. And then it's really easy there to set up. It's kind of a one time thing and then you're all set. But then once you have that conversion tracking set up then this is what your campaigns are going to start looking like when you open up your Facebook ad manager. So you'll see here in one recent campaign we spent $418.98 and that has 349 conversions and this was a product sale and the checkout value was $97. That's how much the product we were selling was. So we spent $418 in ads that generated 349 buyers at $97 so that's $33,853. So that's a pretty good return and we can see which ads are performing and which ads down here are actually giving us the conversions and then pause the ones that aren't working and then increase and scale up the ads that are giving us the conversions. So that's the benefit of using the conversion pixel tracking that Facebook already has built in. You just need to set it up once and then you are good to go. So we've gone over your audience really defining who you're going after and then we've gone over the ads that you create within Facebook. You've got your landing page set up. Now the fourth and final piece of the puzzle here is your conversion mechanism. So what we've seen work really well on Facebook is a rich video and what this does is that it warms up and captures the attention of the people that you are targeting. They're on Facebook, they're hanging out, they're looking at pictures of the friends and family and of the barbecue the other day and then all of a sudden they see your ad and they see this image that just jumps out to them and now they click on the link and they're in a totally different place. And so we find that to be able to have a quick message that then will warm up that traffic from a cold person who wasn't thinking about you two seconds ago to now be interested in what it is that you have to say. And at the end of the day people buy results, period. And so the more results focused you can have this video and have your message be about the results that you can help them get in their life, their body, their business, whatever it is they're selling, then the better off that you're going to be. You also want to, you're also going to use this to build authority and credibility. So what we often do is we will show case study results of our clients and show results of what they've been able to achieve. And so you could showcase potentially some of your clients and what you are doing and how you can help them get results. Now we've seen this conversion funnel work with video sales letter funnels. We've seen it work with case study funnels, with webinar funnels, tele-seminar, evergreen funnels. You know, there really are a lot of different options. So depending on what kind of funnel that you have or if it's going to be a phone call or a call to action is going to be have somebody come into your office, whatever it's going to be, we've seen this work incredibly well. The phase two of that conversion funnel is in the follow-up sequence because you captured that lead initially and now you can continue to follow up with them, sending them targeted messages whether it's email or whether it's blog posts, whether it's videos or whether it's direct mail that you're sending and then you're bringing them constantly back to your website over and over and over and bringing them back to the same offer that you had. So that's the boomerang effect that now you can target them not only with ads but with email, you can target them with retargeting ads and really the more touches from the more places, the better that you're going to be because it usually takes multiple interactions from cold lead to become warmer and warmer and then ultimately be a buyer. You'll definitely get people right off the bat that are ready right now. There's a certain percentage of people that are ready today for your message but then there's a certain percentage of people that are going to need a little bit more time and they're going to come around and as long as you are in their world and they think of you when that time comes and you send the right email at the right time or have the right ad in front of them, then they're going to be able to respond at that time and get on board with you. So as you see here, we've gone over, you target the right audience first and then you set up the right ad, you've got your landing page just created and then you have the right conversion mechanism in place. Now the last step is just to optimize and scale. And really this separates the men from the boys and the small hinges swings the large door. So it's in the details and in the numbers that you're going to be looking at and seeing, okay, how much are you spending per lead and how much does it cost you to acquire a buyer. Once you have those kind of numbers, then you can see, hey, if it costs me $2 to acquire a buyer and I make $10 on average per lead, then I want to spend as much money as I can if for every $2 I spend I make a $10 back, I'm just going to turn up that ad budget so that I can scale more and more and more. So again, turn in $1 into $2 or $5 or $20 or $50 or even more, that's really where it gets fun and that's where the casino royale effect kind of comes into play is where you're then you're just playing with house money and then your advertising is paying more and more and more for itself and that's where it gets really fun. Now a few last kind of case studies here, we had one client, his numbers, he spent $2,000 in ads and generated $72,000 in sales in two weeks and he said, generated $72,000 in sales, $31,000 in real cash, should we increase our ad spend yet? And that was the message that he sent over to me and I sent back a reply and said, absolutely, go ahead and turn it up. Those are good numbers, definitely want to turn that up. I've had a client that spent $400 in ads and made $59,000 in sales in one week. Another client that made from less than $100 in ads and made $52,137 in two weeks from $106 qualifiably. So again, you don't have to have a ton of leads if they are the right leads and that's why we really focus on precise targeting and really focusing on who are your hyperactive clients that you're going after. And then another client here that made $108,000 in one month. So these are some of the results that you can get with Facebook advertising. Facebook is an incredible tool to be able to generate leads. That's really what we look at it as, it is a regeneration machine and if you know how to convert those leads into sales profitably, then really you've got a license to print money, you've got a money print machine on your hat. And I know that we wanted to have some time here to open up for some Q&A. So David, I'll turn it back to you here and we'll see if people have some questions. I'm happy to stick around and answer as many questions as people have here and help you guys get started here with your Facebook advertising. Fantastic, fantastic. Great information. We really appreciate it. Okay, so I do have some questions here from folks. And by the way, if you have a question all you need to do is enter it into the question box and I will see the questions and then I will ask them. So if you've got questions, now is the time to ask them. All right, so the first question comes from George Terry from Vancouver. He says, we sell industrial equipment, consumable items, filters as well as large capital equipment, complete wash water and wastewater treatment packages. How can Facebook be most effectively used when promoting industrial equipment? So industrial equipment, I guess the question is like, who is your customer? Is it a B2B sale that you're selling to other businesses? Yeah, I would assume it's a B2B sale. Okay, all right. So that one might be a little bit trickier, but what you would want to do is you would want to look at who are the types of businesses that would be buying this industrial equipment and buying these filters and then see where they are on Facebook. There are a lot of industries that are on Facebook. There are some industries that aren't on Facebook. And so with that, you would just need to do a little bit of competitive intelligence research initially to see if they are on Facebook. If there are, I would look for groups. If there are any groups that where those kind of businesses congregate, if there are any fan pages or similar products and competitors out there, that would be the first thing that I would look at in the first step to see. And then the other thing that you can do is you could just open up the Facebook Ads Manager and start typing in the type of filters or the type of products that you're selling and the type of businesses that you're going after and see if they show up. A lot of them do. This one, you just have to type it in and see and if it does come up awesome. Then you could, I would imagine it would be a fairly targeted list and for a smaller demographic and then you could just target right to them. So I've got, and I really don't know the answer to this, so this isn't a setup question, but isn't it possible, like let's say I find out that I can find out for sure the purchasing agent's name or the president's name of the company, whoever does the purchasing or makes a final decision on buying these things. If I can find that person's name and they're on Facebook, can I specifically target ads just to them? Yeah, you totally can. You can create, you can go to that person and find what their Facebook ID is and it's like an eight or a ten digit number and then create that. That's in the custom audience. When I was talking about custom audiences, you could create a list of people that make up that custom audience and then say it's 50 people or 100 people, then you could run ads just to those targeted people. The other thing that you could do is if you have direct mail lists and you have email or phone numbers for these people, then you could upload those lists into Facebook as a custom audience and run ads to that custom audience as well. So fantastic. Again, I think that's a great strategy. Because I think what we forget is that we can target individuals. You don't have to do mass advertising all the time, guys. You can target individuals. The late Chet Holmes had a strategy called his Dream 100 and he would specifically target 100, it could be 50, it could be 200, but something manageable who you could really do customized campaigns for. So that's something that you can absolutely do here as well. Guys, one of the questions that we're getting from a number of folks is could you just go through and just lay out in an A to Z fashion, literally step by step what someone should do first, what they should do second, what they should do third, not the actual technology. Yeah, so the first campaign up, again, you want to go out and research your audience. So who is it that you are going after? And that's one of the first things that Facebook is going to ask you as you create your ad is who do you want to go after? You don't want to go after all 180 million people in the US on Facebook. You want to go after a targeted subset, right? So what are their ages, gender? What are the things that they like? And I kind of had a list a little bit earlier of other fan pages that they might like or magazines that they read or books that they read or authors that they follow. So you're going to get it next week then. Yeah, so you guys will have the landing pages next week. And those of you who want to get those set up, then you're ready to create the ad. And so there are really, you need an image for the ad. You need a headline. And then you need a description or that goes in the body part of the ad. Right? And so those are the three things that you need in order to create the ad itself. And you can pull, typically what we do is we'll pull the headline that's on the landing page and we'll use that same copy in the ad so that it's congruent. Right? So we have the same headline on the ad, then they click on the ad, they go to a landing page and it has the same headline. They know they're still in the right place and they're responding and not getting like a totally different message or something. Right? And then you can take, you know, if you have like bullet points on your landing page or like a sub-headline, you can take that copy and use that in the body part of your ad as well. So once you have those three things in place, you know, you have your audience, you have your landing page and then you can create that ad within Facebook. And then Facebook will take it away. You set your budget, you know, if you want to spend $10 a day or $20 a day or $100 a day and then from there you'll start getting, you know, people that are going to be clicking over and looking at that and then you can track the numbers and see, okay, how many people did I have that clicked the ad? Just for me, if they're typing on a laptop or a desktop computer. And that's the same. You know, desktop or laptop computers, those are synonymous. Those are the same thing. So, yeah, there's lots of people. We get conversions every single day from a lot of our campaigns that are desktop. So there is more and more people that are showing up on mobile. That's absolutely true. Mobile is growing like crazy right now. And the advertisers that are tapping into mobile right now are really, they're crushing it. They can do really well, but mobile is a different animal in and of itself. So that's why when we first take on clients, we recommend that they stick with desktop. First, get desktop to work. It's kind of the baby steps, right? Get desktop to work. That's the easy step. And then you can take it to the next level with mobile because there's some more things that you need to take into account with mobile and responsiveness and things like that. But you definitely do want to do mobile, but it's just like a two-step process. Do desktop first, get it to work, then do mobile. We find a lot of people, if they go to mobile too soon or ride out of the gates, they'll blow a lot of their budget and waste it just because they're not ready for it and they're not optimized for mobile. So that's the only thing. Yeah, yeah, great answer. Cindy asked, what is a starting monthly budget for Facebook ads? Many of our local internet marketing people for hire have a $500 to $1,000 minimum. Yeah, so when you go onto Facebook, the minimum that you can create a campaign for is $10 a day. The minimum that we recommend to our clients who are first starting out is we tell them to plan on budgeting $100 to $200 per day. It just depends on what your budget is and how much you want to spend. Those are the kind of clients that we usually work with are spending $100 to $200 a day to start out with. But the minimum that Facebook requires you to set up a campaign is $10 a day. Anything beyond that you can add on. Yeah, and again, so my personal opinion is I would not be hiring an internet company that's going to make you spend a minimum of $2,000 to do it because at least initially do it yourself. Do it yourself. The late Gary Halbert once had a saying that he once taught that you should really be doing, and he was talking about direct mail, doing everything yourself at least for your first campaign, including writing the sales letter, folding up the sales letter, putting it in the envelope, addressing it, putting the stamp on it, putting it in the mail. Because this point was you really want to know the process before you hire somebody to do it for you. It's the same thing with copyrighting. That's why we taught you in this program how to write some pretty decent copy. So even if you hire somebody later on, you know how it works. Again, there's three steps that they laid out for you. That's all you need to do. And I would echo that, what you're saying, Dave, there, that you will learn more spending your first $100 on Facebook than going through any course or spending hours going through training or anything like that. You will learn more actually doing the process yourself the first time and spending your first $100. And maybe it won't return you anything. Maybe you won't make any sales from that first $100. But that $100 will be probably one of the best investments you can make in your business. If you use it as a learning opportunity to then be able to, okay, how can I do this better and better and better? And if you end up bringing on someone that does it for you, then you'll know the process and you'll know how it really works and where you need help. Yeah, again, so just go through and do it. And here's a question from Brian. Brian says, why don't all Facebook business pages show up when you are trying to target them in the ads manager? Yeah, that is a great question, Brian. Facebook is evolving its platform, really. I mean, it's still the Wild West days in terms of Facebook advertising. It's still pretty new. And so, it's been evolving some of those things sometimes when you can't target a fan page just because it doesn't have enough fans. So, it just doesn't have enough fans to register on Facebook's radar. So, if it only has a few hundred or sometimes even a few thousand, it may or may not show up. Facebook has been actually, they've made some adjustments and some changes over the past two or three months. And there were some Facebook fan pages that previously we couldn't target that now we are able to target. And so, Facebook is, they're improving their algorithm in terms of what you can target and the targeting options. And so, you may want to check again if it's been a little bit since you have checked and you weren't able to. It could be available now or it just might be too small of a page. And maybe there are some other pages that are a little bit larger that you could target. So, that would be the alternative I would think of is maybe Brainstalk's other similar pages and see if you could target those instead. Here, I've got two interesting questions. I think you're from the same person, but let me give you both of them because they're both really good ones. I don't understand how uploading phone numbers allows you to target ads on Facebook. You're not calling them, so what's the point of the phone number? Great question. So, Facebook is one of the largest databases. Facebook gets, I mean if you think about all the information that Facebook gets from us, they have our email address, they have where we're living, who we're in relationships with, who we're related to, all that kind of stuff. And many of us who connect with our phones, they also have our phone number. And so, if you upload a list of phone numbers, then Facebook will cross-check its data lists and see which phone numbers match up to which people. And then at the end, it won't register all of the people, all the phone numbers certainly. There's probably going to be numbers in it that Facebook says we don't recognize this one. But it will register quite a few of them, and we see 40 to 50 percent match rate when we're uploading those kind of lists. And then you can advertise to those people that Facebook matches the phone number with actual users, and that's how it works. Fantastic. I mean think about how cool that is. I mean, you know, I want to, if we go back, you guys, to like, you know, direct marketers like from the 30s and 40s, who none of this was available, they were still making millions of dollars, but think about what people had to do, what they had to do to get that money, right? And this makes it so freaking easy. Oh, it's incredible. Yeah. I mean, can you imagine what those old-time guys would be doing with this? I mean, they'd all be billionaires. I mean, they were billionaires back then sending out letters and placing ads in newspapers and people have to cut out a coupon and put it in an envelope and send it to Jack. I mean, it's just insane. This is so good. This is really some of the best targeting, like, some of the best targeting that we've ever been able to do is really on Facebook up to this point. So yeah, absolutely. Here's a related question to what we just said. How does Facebook compare to pay-per-click Google advertising? Which is the best bang for your buck if you had to choose? Well, I know what you guys are going to say. I mean, well, we got the Facebook experts here, so. Right, right, yeah. We choose Facebook. You know, a lot of, Google has had a really similar growth pattern to what Facebook is going through right now, right? So Google initially, in the heydays of Google, the clicks were really cheap. People could get one cent, two cent clicks, like super cheap, and they were just crushing it on Google. And now, a ton of advertisers and business came in and they've driven up the costs and the prices of Google advertising. And some key words, you'll spend 10, 20, 30, 40 up to $100 per click in really hyper-competitive markets. So that is financially, you know, inhibitive for a lot of business to be able to spend that kind of money on Facebook, on Google, sorry. On Facebook, we're still in the wild west days. So it is still relatively very inexpensive for you to advertise on Facebook. We're getting, on average, clicks on Facebook in the U.S. for anywhere from 40 cents to 80 cents per cost per click is what we're getting. And we're getting leads for anywhere from $3 to $5 per lead. So I don't know how that compares to Google advertising. If Google advertising is working for you, don't stop it. Keep on going, all right? I'm not telling you to stop anything, but maybe you can see if you can add Facebook, see if it does better for you, or at least supplements and can add to what you're doing currently on Google too. Yeah, absolutely. Again, remember, and maybe I didn't even tell you this, but let me give you the, you know, the J. Bram is a brilliant market, something called the Greek Parthenon analogy. And the diving board and the Greek Parthenon. So if you draw a diving board and you think about a diving board, a diving board has what? One pillar that holds it up. And so what is the nature of a diving board? It goes up and it goes down. And if you knock away that one pillar underneath your diving board, then the entire diving board comes crashing down. So the diving board is your business. The pillars under your diving board are the ways that you generate leads. Now I want you to think of a Greek Parthenon. The Greek Parthenon has many, many, many different pillars holding up, holding it up, right? And so if one of those pillars goes away, well, it may have an impact, but your entire business doesn't come crumbling down. So what you want to do is you want to use as many strategies as you can effectively use and you want to use all of them. So you need to determine, now this is beyond the scope of this program. So please don't ask questions regarding what I'm about to say because it's really beyond the scope of the program. But if you just think about it simple, you need to determine how much you are willing to spend to acquire a customer, all right? You need to have that number in mind and it should be more than you probably think you should, all right? And then you want to use every strategy you possibly can that either comes this less than that number or equals that number. That's how you judge whether or not you should use the strategy. You don't just use the least expensive one. Use all of them that are at that number or below that number. And that way you're going to build a very stable business and have leads coming in from different sources. And that's what we're teaching you how to do here. So this comes from Alyssa. Hey, this is actually a great question. Alyssa asks, what is a formula for an ad that works? Is there a particular formula that works? Formula for an ad, you know, we find for headlines that results work really good. So being able to show results in advance and questions work really well. So like if you're, again, kind of going back to the chiropractor example, you know, back pain question mark or, you know, do you have neck pain question mark? And then, you know, click here to get a free analysis or a free whatever. Giving anything free with what you're doing with your landing pages is going to be great. Providing case studies or results of how you can help them. Every industry is a little bit different, like if you're an agent. In real estate, you want to be able to give them either a list of homes that they're looking for or to find out how much that their home is worked. For some of our health insurance and insurance agents that we work with, we tell them, you know, hey, click here to find out how you can save 50 to 80 percent on your monthly insurance bills. And have a free calculator there for them, though they can submit. So free things work great. Questions in the headline work tremendously to use numbers specifically. You know, if you have some specific numbers as far as like how much they could save or how much that they're going to get, those are things that work really, really well for us in terms of ad copy. Fantastic. Yeah, that's great. Now, we're getting a bunch of questions from people who are saying, well, how do I target this person? How do I target that person? We can't go through and so they've taught you how to target. So let me just give you an example and this is the last one I'm going to answer about, you know, specific people you're trying to target. So this guy who's trying to target men who are in their 40s who are engineers, I mean, that seems like it would be pretty simple just based upon what you've already taught us. Am I missing something or is that a difficult thing to do? Yeah, yeah, no, just go on in the Facebook Ads Manager and click, you know, men who are between the ages of 40 and 49 who like engineering or like an engineering association or like engineering, you know, mechanical engineering or whatever type of engineering it is, different Facebook pages or interests. And then Facebook will tell you right there how many people that fit that criteria that you just typed in. And if it's, you know, maybe 200,000, maybe it's a million, you know, how many people, you know, it will tell you exactly how many people is in that group of potential prospects that you're targeting. Yeah, again, so, you know, I think a lot of folks or a few folks are way over complicating this. Just, you may want to go through the session again, but they literally laid out, can you guys just one more time lay it out for someone to get their first ad up? And that's all we want you to do here is to get your first ad up. Can you just lay out your steps again, the three steps that you have? You bet, you bet, yeah. So you're going to want to find out who it is your audience is the first thing. So figure out who it is that you are targeting with your ad and you can do that simply by going into the Facebook Ads Manager, clicking a few buttons, you know, selecting, you know, US or the state or the zip codes or, you know, the gender and then typing in some of the interests. Then you're going to have your landing page, which you're going to be getting live next week here with you guys. And then you're going to take whatever you've created on your landing page, the headline and some of the copy and then based on that headline and copy that you're using on your landing page, you're going to create an ad out of it. So you're going to write, you know, take the same exact headline that you have on your landing page and put it in your ad. And then you can take some of the copy and bullet points and make that the body copy of your ad. And then you just need to find an image that you can do, that you can use that would pop out, you know, bright colors work really well on Facebook. So you know, reds and oranges and yellows and greens, those are, you know, any bright vibrant colors that you can use of faces specifically, where it will work really good. So if you want to do, you know, picture yourself or picture some of your clients or you just need an image and you take that image, you combine it with the headline that you already have and then you will set your budget and click go. So audience ad and landing page, those are the three things. Yeah, and landing page is going to be done next week. The actual headline is already done. So, and who your prospect is, is already done. All you're going to do is go to Facebook and enter that stuff in. You know, Jackie Boyd Turk from GKIC, you know, just sent me a Skype and he says, you know, some folks are floundering in the tech aspect of this. And we just want you to grasp the concept of doing it and laying it out. So guys, how hard is the tech when they go to Facebook and they go to do this, is it a difficult process? It doesn't seem like it's a difficult process. No, no, it's really pretty intuitive as you go into a Facebook, it will lead to a step by step through it in terms of creating the ad. And it may take you a few extra minutes the first time in doing it. But again, guys, you know, setting up that first ad and spending your first hundred dollars, you know, that's going to be more valuable than hours you could spend, you know, studying and trying to learn about it. Just get in there and actually do it and then you'll get better and better at it as you do more. Yeah, great great answer. This is a good question coming from Phil. He says, do all sponsored ads we run get seen by our target prospect or is there a bidding system like PaperClick? Yeah, there is a bidding system. PaperClick, so you're bidding in an open market with other people who are targeting similar users, right? And so that, the bid will actually fluctuate based on what other people are bidding. And then, but it's also fluctuating based on what, you know, what engagement you're getting on your ad. So again, like we mentioned, the more engagement that you can have on your ad with people liking and commenting and sharing, then the better off that you're going to be. So there is a bidding system and it's going to fluctuate, but we've seen typically for US traffic again, it's in the 40 cents to 80 cents range. And as you do more and more advertising with Facebook, that cost will actually go down. So it may start up with your very first ad and it may say, you know, Facebook will have a suggested bid range initially. And we always stay within that suggested bid range because we want the ad to show. And so, you know, Facebook initially says, you know, we need you to bid between a dollar to a dollar fifty, then maybe put like a dollar twenty-five. And then Facebook, that doesn't mean that you will spend a dollar twenty-five per click, it just means that you're bidding up to that. And as Facebook shows your ad and sees that you have a good ad, that people are responding to that you picked a good target audience, then Facebook is going to lower that cost per click so that you're not going to spend anywhere near what your bid is, but you're going to spend, you know, in that 40 to 80 cents range if you're bidding in the US. If you're targeting, say, a hundred thousand people, all hundred thousand people aren't necessarily going to see your ad because, you know, maybe they're on vacation and they didn't open up Facebook for, you know, the next two weeks. Maybe they won't see your ad, but, you know, the majority of them and the hyperactive people will definitely see that ad, especially if they're bidding well. Well, it's no different than placing, there's no place, there's no different than placing an ad in a magazine. A hundred percent of the subscribers aren't going to see it. Yep. I mean, is he exactly? Or any type or anything else? Yeah. Yeah, I mean, it's the exact same thing. It's the exact same thing. Now, by the way, everybody, they were very kind enough to provide their actual slides. And if you're on the page, and you should be, who are watching this presentation, all you do is click on the link and you can actually download the slides. And please don't make it more difficult than it is. And then as they said over and over, they must have said it three to five times, go and set up your first campaign. Now, don't go set it up until we get the landing page set up because that doesn't make any sense, all right? But go and set up your first campaign. And I'm telling you, if you spend a hundred bucks on setting up your campaign, you're going to know how to do this. All right? A lot of the questions that we're getting confusion is just because you haven't really gone and done it yet, when you go and do it, it's all going to become crystal clear.
